summary.overall: Chapter 2 was reviewed in 73 bounded source-grounded shards. Proposed changes: 364 replacements, 0 splits, and 55 removals.
patterns: Paragraph-number references in stems teach document trivia rather than operational knowledge (1 question affected).

Thin explanations that restate the answer rather than teaching the controlling rule persist in 3 items, though the source blocks for those concepts provide minimal further explanatory material that would permit expansion without inventing unsupported rationale.
Cross-format duplication exists between one true/false question and one conditional_rule_check activity that test the identical emergency-deviation rule with identical true/false judgment.
Source fidelity is strong — all items correctly reflect the source blocks of paragraph 2-1-10.
Cross-format reinforcement is adequate: second-aircraft report, standby activation, minimum recording data, and GPS/WAAS advisory phraseology each appear in at least two formats.
Most explanations are accurate restatements of the source. Explanations for purely procedural items cannot teach a deeper rationale because the source itself only states the required actions.
The phraseology_builder activity is well-constructed with a verified correct sequence, appropriate word bank with plausible distractor words, and a clear situational prompt.
Step 4 of subparagraph a (normal operation reported after standby activation) is not covered by any item in this shard — a coverage gap for the orchestrator.
Thin explanations that restate the correct answer rather than teaching the controlling source rule (5 items across both paragraphs)
One explanation exposes paragraph-structure knowledge referencing 'Subparagraph c'
Core answers and distractor quality are consistently accurate across all items
Paragraph 2-1-12 has strong cross-format reinforcement with questions, activities, and flashcards covering each application rule
Scenario-based activity 00442c5e effectively applies the exclusive-support rule to a realistic trainee interaction

Cross-format reinforcement is well executed — beacon-code rules, join-up conditions, separation responsibility, and formation control each appear in at least two formats (question + activity + flashcard).

Thin explanations recur where the source provides little elaboration. When the source is a terse directive (e.g., 'Control formation flights as a single aircraft'), there is no additional source material to teach beyond restating the rule.
Answer-length cue on the join-up-conditions question is a real defect — the correct answer is 26 words while the longest distractor is 12 words.
No paragraph-location trivia, no underspecified prompts, no malformed reverse cards, and no near-duplicate items within the same format.
Explanations across several items merely rephrase the correct answer without connecting it to the operational principle or the broader requirement structure.
Paragraph-location scaffolding appears in one stem referencing a subparagraph letter; the other items avoid this pattern.
Answer-length cue detected in two items where the correct answer is substantially longer than all distractors, making the answer identifiable by surface features.
One activity duplicates a question — same concept, same multiple-choice retrieval mode — adding no new learning value.
Distractor quality is generally good; wrong answers represent plausible operational misunderstandings rather than obvious throwaways.

Paragraph-location scaffolding persists in two items: flashcard f844d98a front cites '2-1-16' and activity 87fb7a67 asks whether the paragraph covers three enumerated operations rather than whether a rule applies in a given situation
Question 83dc4068 has a correct answer nearly twice as long as any distractor, creating an answer-length cue
Flashcard 1857ea79 has a one-word back that does not support retrieval
True/false question 1dab0fb2 duplicates the concept already tested by MC question 83dc4068 without adding retrieval diversity
Core answers and rule correspondence are accurate across all items; no source-fidelity errors were found
Cross-format coverage is adequate: each of the three subparagraphs and the note appears in at least two formats
Answer-length cues affect 3 of 6 multiple-choice items: the correct answer is systematically more detailed and wordier than the three distractors, telegraphing correctness.

Thin explanations that merely restate the answer or cite a subparagraph number rather than teaching the operational rule appear in 3 items — one fill-in-the-blank question, one true/false question, and one scope-check activity.
One near-duplicate concept (monitor on ground, 2-1-17e) appears as both a true/false question and a conditional_rule_check activity with overlapping question text.

Flashcards are generally well-formed: fronts identify the concept, backs are concise, and the thin_back flag on the REMAIN THIS FREQUENCY card is a false positive since exact phraseology recall benefits from a minimal back.

The negative_stem automated flag on the fill-in-the-blank question is a false positive — the stem describes a negative situation (controller does not want a change) but is not a pedagogical negative-stem question.

Several source concepts are underrepresented: ERAM voice communication indicator (2-1-17d), location name omission for same-facility or shared-name tower/TRACON transfers (2-1-17b Note 1 TERMINAL), and the CHANGE TO MY FREQUENCY combined-sector scenario beyond the one flashcard.
Thin explanations that restate the answer verbatim rather than teaching the controlling conditions and source structure — two questions have explanations of only 9 words each.
Good cross-format coverage: all four response types (approve, restricted approval, unable, stand by) appear in at least two formats.
Strong scenario-based activity (5987670c) tests operational judgment with realistic peer-pressure distractor.
Phraseology flashcards are appropriately minimal — the exact phrase is the retrieval target for those card types.
Rote elements (STAND BY definition, APPROVED AS REQUESTED substitution) are tested in formats appropriate for exact recall.
Paragraph-location scaffolding is present in four items that reference '2-1-19' or '2-1-2' in stems or card fronts.
Near-duplicate pairs exist for the wake turbulence application trigger (two questions, two flashcards) and the touchdown-continuation rule (two nearly identical true/false activities).
Thin explanations are common in 2-1-19 items; several simply restate the correct answer or describe the item's structure rather than teaching the operational rule.
2-1-2 items are generally well-constructed with appropriate difficulty calibration, plausible distractors, and suitable activity-type matching.
The situation_action activity for 2-1-2 is a strong example of operational decision-making assessment.
Most items correctly avoid paragraph-location scaffolding; the one violator (d5d64b49) references '2-1-20' in its front, a pattern the automated flag correctly identified.
Answer-length cues are rare but present when a compound correct statement sits against single-clause distractors; the knowledge_check activity 9cc428ad demonstrates this.
Phraseology recall cards for Super and Heavy (9384e10a, f4bdb90f) have single-word backs flagged as thin_back, but the exact word is the legitimate retrieval target — no intervention needed.
The phraseology_builder activity (dfedb565) provides word-bank sequencing that correctly exercises the full advisory structure including position and direction per the body block.
The true_false question (7fa11b63) uses a near-verbatim source quote which is appropriate for a rule-presence check but offers limited retrieval depth.
Answer-length cue appears twice (d9abad8b, b6cf6da8) when a compound correct answer sits against short single-clause distractors; the pattern is structural rather than factual.
The phraseology_builder activity (0546c9ef) uses whole-number 'TEN' for the mileage value 10, but the source example explicitly uses group form 'one zero miles'; the word bank lacks 'ZERO' entirely.

The fill_blank question (99228ece) degenerated to a single-choice format that tests nothing beyond recognizing one word; converting to multiple_choice with phraseology distractors makes it a meaningful recognition task.

Three flashcards (cbf21559, 24ea0f62, 51bfb6c9) have backs that are either grammatically awkward, too vague to serve as a retrieval target, or fail to teach the actual phraseology the front asks about.
The remaining 11 items are educationally coherent and faithful to the source, with no paragraph-location scaffolding, plausible distractors, and appropriate difficulty levels.
Coverage is concentrated on the radar-identified and non-radar-identified advisory content; the Mode C altitude-reporting subparagraph is lightly exercised by only one flashcard.

Example trivia masquerading as substance: question 0a5fd2ef and flashcard 8b375362 test which UA type appeared in an illustrative phraseology example — this is document recall, not ATC competence. The fill-in-blank question 9010baa5 similarly tests memory of a specific word in a bird advisory example.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: questions 4117c4d4 and ca311510 open with 'Under 2-1-23' and questions cb2ed1db and 9010baa5 cite paragraph numbers in their explanations. This trains paragraph-number recall instead of operational knowledge.

Thin or restatement explanations: questions ce712915 and ca311510 provide explanations that merely restate the correct answer, and ca311510's explanation cites a paragraph location instead of teaching the rule.

Invented rationale in explanations: flashcard ee565d08 asks 'why' the paragraph distinguishes categories when the source never states a reason, and activity cfc01758 adds 'help pilots evaluate the hazard' which is not stated in the source.

Cross-format duplication: activity 9101e1d1 (knowledge_check) duplicates question 3d2534a8 (multiple_choice) with nearly identical stems and answer sets — same retrieval mode with no added educational value.

Weak distractors: question 73c93a5f uses distractors with giveaway language ('never', 'regardless of whether') and implausible options (registration numbers, lat/lon) that fail to represent realistic operational mistakes.
Phraseology-builder activities (4ce3cf2c, 58d510af) are well-constructed with accurate target phrases and word banks matching the source examples.
The detail-set flashcards (b28542a6, 605b334c) and operational-trigger flashcards (8ef87d21, 9ed75308) are concise, accurate, and test substantive ATC knowledge.

Pervasive paragraph-location scaffolding: all 11 questions cite paragraph numbers in stem, explanation, or both. The stems include 'under 2-1-24,' 'in 2-1-25,' or 'Under 2-1-27,' and explanations repeat 'Per 2-1-XX' across every question. This substitutes document navigation for operational recall.

Answer-length cues in knowledge-check activities: the two activities for 2-1-24 and 2-1-26 have correct answers that are dramatically longer than their distractors, making the correct choice identifiable by format rather than knowledge. The 2-1-24 activity answer is a full clause with prepositional chains while distractors are terse 'Only when...' fragments; the 2-1-26 activity answer enumerates six categories while distractors name single narrow conditions.

Thin or restatement explanations: the fill-in-the-blank questions for 2-1-25 (FINAL APPROACH FIX) and 2-1-27 (CONTACT) have explanations that merely state which word is correct, plus a paragraph citation. The phraseology-builder for CHECK WHEELS DOWN has an explanation that simply repeats the target phrase ('The correct transmission is CHECK WHEELS DOWN'). These do not teach the operational principle behind the phraseology.

The three flashcards (2-1-25 wheels-down trigger, 2-1-26 supervisory conditions, 2-1-27 deviation notification timing) are clean, concise, and test substantive operational recall. No paragraph references, no length cues, well-scoped fronts and backs.

The phraseology-builder activity for the Brasher notification (2-1-27) is well-constructed with a complete word bank and accurate correct sequence matching the source phraseology. The spot-the-error activity correctly identifies the POSSIBLE/PROBABLE substitution.
No item tests a paragraph illustration or example as if it were normative content — all questions address the actual operational rules.
No items invent safety rationales, policy purposes, or operational consequences absent from the source blocks.

Paragraph-location scaffolding is the dominant defect: 10 of 16 items embed 'Under 2-1-28' or 'Under 2-1-29' in stems, and 10 include 'Per 2-1-28a' style references in explanations, teaching document structure instead of operational procedure.

Thin or trivia explanations: 4 items have explanations that either restate the answer without teaching the rule, or reduce to paragraph-location trivia ('That statement appears in 2-1-28c'). Two of these provide zero instructional value.

Sub-paragraph letter mismatch: two question explanations reference '2-1-28d' for content that the packet source blocks label as paragraph 'c', suggesting a source-edition misalignment in the generation process.

Answer-length cue: one multiple-choice question has a correct answer substantially longer than its distractors because it must list three resumption conditions; distractor expansion partially mitigates but does not eliminate the length differential.

Format misuse: one table_lookup activity asks which table is referenced rather than requiring the student to use the table to make an operational decision — it teaches document-structure trivia under the table_lookup activity type.
Fill-blank punctuation: one item's stem omits a comma present in the published phraseology example, introducing a minor source-fidelity discrepancy.

Paragraph-location scaffolding in explanations is the most frequent defect, affecting four questions and one flashcard across all three paragraphs. Explanations begin with 'Per 2-1-30a,' 'Per 2-1-30b,' or 'Per 2-1-31' instead of teaching the operational content directly.

Answer-length cuing affects two items (one question, one activity) that ask when TAWS separation responsibility resumes. The correct answer must convey three distinct conditions while distractors are short, single-clause statements. The correct answer becomes identifiable by length alone.

Near-duplication: a knowledge_check activity (897236f2) tests the same operational fact as a multiple-choice question (f8d80300) using the same retrieval mode and nearly identical stems. This provides no additional educational value.
Paragraph-location scaffolding on a flashcard front: card 8c33eb5a asks 'What principle drives the choices in 2-1-3?' teaching document structure instead of the operational principle.

Flashcard back ambiguity: card 57a31a2d uses 'or...or...and' phrasing that obscures which TAWS separation-resumption conditions require the controller to observe reestablished separation. The source requires observation only for conditions 2 and 3, not condition 1.
Five of six procedural-preference questions (2-1-3) are well-formed: self-contained stems, plausible distractors, and accurate if thin explanations. No intervention needed.

The situation_action activity (19617ad7) is a strong item: it presents an operational conflict between radar availability and vertical-separation advantage, requires applying the procedural-preference rule, and uses plausible incorrect interpretations as distractors.
Most items are source-accurate and have plausible distractors grounded in operational mistakes from the source notes.
Coverage is concentrated on medical-priority triggers (MEDEVAC verbal ID and AIR EVAC/HOSP verbal request), with multiple items testing the same fact in functionally identical formats.
The paragraph-location scaffold in item 7c1286e9 is the only instance of document-trivia scaffolding in this shard; other items reference operational triggers, not paragraph numbers.
Most priority categories in 2-1-4 — presidential, SAR, interceptor, FLYNET, SAMP, SCOOT, TEAL/NOAA, Flight Check, NRP, diverted, and FALLEN HERO — have zero test coverage in this shard.
The situation_action activity (d5ed597c) is the strongest item: it embeds the medical-priority distinction in a realistic training scenario with a trainee misconception.

Strengths: The expedite/immediately distinction is well-tested across definition recall, scenario application, and reasoning comparison. Distractors are plausible operational mistakes (confusing the two definitions, escalating automatically, omitting urgency words). The situation-action activity with embedded reason inclusion is particularly effective.

Thin explanation: One question (33ea24d3) explains only which word fits rather than restating both source thresholds. The current explanation ('That description matches expedite rather than immediately') would fail the validator's minimum-explanation check.

Thin flashcard back: One card (ba676a0c) has a single-word back ('Expedite') without definition context. While the front provides the scenario description, the bare back weakens the reverse retrieval direction.
Automated false positives: Two items (33ea24d3, 8b806f62) carry negative_stem flags but neither actually uses a negative stem — both ask positive 'which word fits' or 'what should you do' questions.
Answer-length cues: two items have correct answers substantially longer than distractors, telegraphing the answer independently of content knowledge.
Thin explanations: one fill-in-the-blank phraseology item restates the answer without connecting to the operational alert context.
Negative-stem true/false: one item presents a prohibited-action statement as a true/false stem, requiring double-negative reasoning.
Concept duplication: a knowledge_check activity and a multiple_choice question both test 'when to issue a safety alert' in identical retrieval mode.
Data quality: a phraseology_builder word bank contains a duplicate entry (ALTITUDE at two indices), creating unnecessary ambiguity in sequence scoring.
Strength: The three core duties of 2-1-7 (determine, provide, relay) are well-covered across questions, activities, and flashcards, giving good cross-format reinforcement.
Strength: Distractors are operationally plausible (e.g. only separation services, only if communications fail completely, must cancel IFR) and test genuine misunderstandings a controller might hold.
Strength: Explanations generally teach the controlling requirement directly, stating what the rule demands without inventing safety rationales or policy purposes.
Strength: Activity type choices (conditional_rule_check, knowledge_check, requirement_check, scope_check) appropriately match the tested skill.
Defect: Two items include explicit paragraph-number references ('the note to 2-1-7', 'Why does 2-1-7 require'), scaffolding document trivia instead of operational knowledge.
Defect: One reasoning-type flashcard fabricates an operational rationale ('instead of rediscovering the problem after handoff') that is absent from the source blocks.
Strong source fidelity — all correct answers match the two source blocks verbatim or in tight paraphrase.
The boundary between minimum fuel advisory and emergency declaration is tested across multiple items, which is the central operational distinction of the paragraph.
One knowledge_check activity near-duplicates a question with nearly identical stems and matching multiple-choice retrieval mode.
Two flashcards exhibit thin or context-light presentation: one back is a bare 'No.', and one front omits the minimum-fuel scenario context.
Paragraph-location scaffolding permeates five items where stems or fronts cite '2-1-9' instead of describing the operational situation.
Cross-format reinforcement exists for the three core concepts (what to report, where to report, timing) but five items need repair before that reinforcement is effective.

The reasoning flashcard invents rationale — it asserts a sequential relationship between reporting and NOTAM classification that the source blocks do not state, and attributes motivation to 'the controller's job' that does not appear in the source.
One flashcard back is a bare acronym ('FSSs.') — technically correct but educationally thin for a retrieval card.
One activity explanation is a tautological restatement rather than instructional content.
One MC question uses implausible distractors (locally invented shorthand, military mission prefixes) that do not represent realistic operational mistakes a controller would make.
One MC question has a correct answer substantially longer than all distractors, creating a reliable answer-length guessing cue.
The authorized abbreviations directive appears in two questions (one MC, one true/false) and one activity, with the true/false question adding no retrieval diversity.
En route recording and box 26 requirements have strong cross-format coverage with well-constructed items, including a difficulty-3 situation_action activity and a reasoning flashcard.

Three of five explanations restate the answer without teaching the complete operational context, though the source blocks are purely procedural and do not provide explanatory rationale beyond the rules themselves.

The PBCT / Computer B rule is covered across three formats (multiple-choice question, knowledge-check activity, procedure flashcard), which provides the cross-format reinforcement the audit findings identified as weak.

All three nonautomated NADIN trigger conditions are covered by at least one item each, and the situation-action activity correctly tests the operational distinction between the Computer B and NADIN transmission paths.
No paragraph-location scaffolding in questions or activities; only one flashcard front references the paragraph number.
Explanations often restate the correct answer verbatim rather than contextualizing the rule with the prescribed phraseology template or operational procedure from the source.
Correct multiple-choice answers are substantially longer than distractors, creating answer-length cues that undermine assessment validity.
The knowledge_check activity duplicates a multiple_choice question with an identical stem, identical correct answer, and overlapping distractors, adding no distinct retrieval mode.
Cross-format reinforcement exists but unevenly; UTM/FDIO coordination and the 30-minute amendment rule are covered by both question and activity formats while other concepts appear only once.
Paragraph-number references embedded in stems (question 5c00bba9, flashcard 6bd944b5) teach document-trivia instead of operational knowledge.
Distractors that are not plausible ATC concepts — e.g., 'request for the next controller's initials,' 'preference for a different company name' — weaken answer defensibility.
Answer-length cue in question 765e1383: the correct answer is more than double the length of each distractor, making it identifiable without content knowledge.
Thin flashcard backs (e.g., 'To FSSs.', 'IFR flight plans.') provide too little substance for effective retrieval practice.
Near-duplicate flashcards (07ac3eef and 4f4d3e44) cover the same retrieval path with only a single-word distinction.
Paragraph-location scaffolding confirmed in one item (2361b03e) — stem references paragraph number '2-2-13' directly, converting a substance question into document-trivia recall.
Answer-length cue confirmed in one item (11647b9b) — the correct answer is roughly twice as long as the longest distractor, revealing the answer without operational knowledge.

All four negative_stem automated flags are false positives. Each flagged item states a positive operational rule with an embedded exception clause (e.g. 'except for flights on mutually agreed airways'), which is standard ATC regulatory language and not a negative-stem construction.

Cross-format reinforcement is present for each sub-rule: domestic lead time (question dde9eed3 + activity dbd9b7ab), international forwarding (question 11647b9b + activity da863882), and return-to-U.S. voice forwarding (question 52b81712 + activity a31e0ad4).
Thin explanation confirmed in one item (2361b03e) — the original explanation restates the answer without teaching the controlling rule.
No flashcards exist for this paragraph. The content is procedural coordination rules without prescribed phraseology, defined terms, or memorized sequences, so flashcard absence is appropriate.

Paragraph-location scaffolding: 6 items embed cross-references like '2-2-6a' or '2-2-14' in stems, backs, explanations, or activity content, teaching document navigation instead of operational procedure.
Thin explanations: 4 items restate the correct answer without teaching the controlling rule, condition, or operational context. Explanations average under 10 words.
Format mismatch: One fill_blank question asks for a numeric answer ('8') with no distractors, testing paragraph trivia rather than operational knowledge.

Negative stem: One question asks which message type is 'listed for Canadian ACC transmission but not in the U.S. list,' obscuring a straightforward operational fact (Canadian ACCs transmit correction messages).
Well-formed items: 5 items (flashcards F1, F3, F4, F5 and activity A2) are correctly structured with self-contained prompts, concise backs, and adequate explanations.

Explanations across the shard tend to restate the answer rather than teach the operational condition or distinction. The source blocks are short and give no stated rationale, so explanations can only describe when the rule applies — but they should still contrast the triggering condition rather than repeat the answer.
Four of five questions and three of five activities use the same source rules in different retrieval modes, providing cross-format reinforcement for the NRP retention/removal distinction.
One fabricated distractor references an unrelated aviation acronym (UTM) imported from outside the chapter.

Question explanations tend to restate the correct answer verbatim rather than contextualising the rule within the broader forwarding framework. The source material is procedural with no stated rationale, limiting how much an explanation can teach beyond the rule itself, but explanations can still connect related source blocks (e.g., linking the EAS FDP exception back to the baseline manual actions).

Activity coverage of block a's baseline forwarding rule is duplicated across knowledge_check (4-choice) and requirement_check (T/F) types. The different activity types represent different retrieval modes and are acceptable, but future generation should avoid producing two activities for the same source statement when coverage of other concepts is thin.

Three automated 'negative_stem' flags are false positives for this paragraph — the items use scope qualifiers like 'except' or 'if/but' to set operational context while asking a positive determination. The conditional_rule_check flag is especially inappropriate since conditional language is inherent to that activity type.
Explanations that reference paragraph structure ('the paragraph is explicitly labeled terminal') instead of operational responsibility.
Self-referential stem language ('this forwarding rule,' 'this VFR forwarding rule') that assumes context unavailable at runtime.
Thin explanations that restate the answer or the fill-in word rather than teaching the controlling rule.
Negative-stem constructions where a positive conditional would be clearer and equally valid.
Intra-format redundancy: the same two source rules are tested multiple times within the same format with minimal retrieval diversity.
Thin explanations that merely restate the correct answer or the source phrase without conveying the operational requirement or its scope.
Paragraph-location scaffolding in one flashcard front explicitly referencing '2-2-4' instead of the operational concept.
Answer-length cueing in the situation-action activity: the correct answer is substantially longer than distractors because it addresses two independent rules, creating a surface cue.

False-positive automated flags: negative_stem was flagged on an activity whose stem is positive ('What should you do?'), and thin_back was flagged on phraseology/definition flashcards whose backs are correctly concise for their retrieval target.
Over-coverage: 14 items (5 questions, 4 activities, 5 flashcards) for a single one-sentence source paragraph creates unavoidable redundancy.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: 3 of 5 flashcards reference the paragraph number ('2-2-5') instead of using operational context in the front.
Thin explanations: Multiple items have explanations that merely restate the correct answer ('The pilot is directed to the appropriate FSS.') rather than teaching the full rule with trigger condition.
Trivial scope/trigger items: Two items (c57cc57c question, 2ca97101 activity) merely restate the rule's scope condition and ask if it applies, contributing no operational learning.
Same-mode duplication: Questions 8a758bf5 and 4b5480e2 are both multiple-choice questions testing the identical operational fact with only minor stem rewording.

Good cross-format reinforcement: The strip-remarks prohibition appears across four formats (question, directive_check, knowledge_check, flashcard). The correctness-and-currency requirement appears across three formats (question, requirement_check, flashcard).

Thin explanations: The explanation for q_1a0c4ac6 restates the answer verbatim ('The information must be correct and up to date.') instead of connecting the requirement to its operational scope in the coordination process.
Answer-length cue: q_d75909ab's correct answer ('The remarks section of flight progress strips') is substantially longer than its distractors, allowing recognition without operational knowledge.
Same-mode duplication: a_057deb75 (knowledge_check with multiple-choice) and q_d75909ab (multiple_choice) test the identical prohibition in equivalent retrieval modes.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: fc_6299408a embeds the paragraph number '2-2-6' in its front, teaching document-location trivia instead of operational recall.
Strong distractor quality: Items with distractors generally contain plausible operational mistakes drawn from adjacent concepts in the same paragraph, making them educationally effective.
Three items (one question, two flashcards) embed paragraph-number references ('2-2-7') in stems or fronts, teaching document trivia instead of operational substance.
Two questions have explanations that merely restate the question rather than teaching the complete rule with its condition and action.
One flashcard pair constitutes a near-duplicate within the same format, asking slightly different fronts for the same retrieval target.

Four of five activities are true/false or best-answer variants rephrasing the same single-sentence rule under different activity type labels. They are factually correct and type-diverse but collectively thin.
The situation_action activity includes a para_context field that recites the source rule, undermining the operational decision the student is supposed to make.
Answer-length cues: full-sentence correct answers paired with terse distractors make two items answerable by length alone.
Near-duplication: two activities test the identical rule via positive and negative rephrasing of the same statement.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: one flashcard front references the paragraph number rather than the operational condition.
Thin explanation: one true/false explanation is a tautology that restates the answer without teaching the controlling rule.
Strength — operational coverage: each of the three forwarding purposes (identify, update ALTRV data, revise prior information) and the scope condition are each tested in at least one format.
Paragraph-location scaffolding appears in five of six flashcards and one question stem, where item prompts name the paragraph number instead of the operational condition.
Four items have explanations that either restate the answer or reference a subparagraph without teaching the controlling principle in adequate detail.
One multiple-choice question has a correct answer two to three times longer than any distractor, creating an answer-length cue that reveals the answer regardless of knowledge.
One fill_blank question duplicates the concept already tested by a stronger multiple-choice question in the same paragraph set, splitting retrieval across two items without adding educational value.

Cross-format coverage of the three core verification items (aircraft identification, assigned altitude, departure or coordination fix time) is solid, appearing in questions, activities, and flashcards.

Explanations tend toward restatement rather than teaching the procedural principle; however, the source blocks for this paragraph state procedures without stated rationales, so procedural restatement is largely appropriate per the harness standard.
One activity explanation invents an unsupported auditability rationale absent from the source blocks.
Two paragraph objects covering the same source material produced a near-duplicate flashcard pair; the second paragraph's flashcard set overlaps the first with only cosmetic wording differences.
Most distractors are plausible operational mistakes drawn from the source procedures (erase vs. X-delete, line-through timing, adjacent placement).
Several uncovered source statements (manual strip format conformance, slant-line zero/underline-S requirements, weather-data zero marking) remain unaddressed by any item.
Thin explanations are the most common defect across questions — the explanation restates the answer rather than teaching the controlling authorization or condition.
Paragraph-location scaffolding appears in one question stem and one activity (question_text, source_label, and explanation), substituting document trivia for operational context.
One multiple-choice question has a dramatic answer-length cue: the correct choice is 3× longer than each distractor, making it identifiable without content knowledge.
The flashcard set is structurally sound but one card has a context-free back that reduces the retrieval target to a bare phrase with no connection to its recording function.

Three source concepts receive excessive item coverage (locally approved identifiers: 5 items; control status: 4 items; CAF: 3 items), while plain language markings and control information symbols receive near-zero coverage.
Thin explanations that restate the answer (e.g., 'That entry belongs in block 15') rather than referencing TBL 2-3-1 and stating the operational content the block records.
Answer-length cueing where the correct multiple-choice answer is noticeably longer than distractors that use absolute-language patterns like 'Only...'.
Cross-format overrepresentation: block 24 and the latitude/longitude substitution rule each appear in 3 item formats (question + flashcard + activity), exceeding the 2-format ceiling.
Near-duplicate across entity types: a knowledge_check activity and a multiple-choice question both ask about latitude/longitude substitution in space 25 in identical retrieval mode.
Paragraph-location scaffolding: a table_lookup question text includes '2-3-2' instead of labeling the source as 'the en route flight progress strip block table'.

Thin explanations across flag-definition items restate the one-to-one mapping without including the operational precondition ('selectable for aircraft whose flight plans contain the required equipment qualifiers') that the source explicitly provides in Block 4.
Paragraph-number scaffolding in the table_lookup activity's question_text, source_label, and task fields teaches location trivia rather than source-use skills.
Reduced separation flags (M, W, D, 3, R) are well-distributed across question, activity, and flashcard formats, providing cross-format reinforcement within this shard.
The situation_action activity is a model item: it embeds two distinct operational rules in a realistic multi-controller scenario with plausible distractors.
Three replacement decisions: two questions and one activity, all with minor defects in balance, explanation depth, or paragraph-location scaffolding.
The correct answer in the space 9A omission question is significantly longer and more specific than any distractor, creating an answer-length cue.

Two items carry thin-explanation flags; one (Q4) is genuinely thin because the explanation merely repeats the answer, while the flashcard thin_back flags are false positives for precise single-term retrieval targets.

One table-lookup activity embeds the paragraph number '2-3-4' in the question text, which teaches paragraph-location trivia rather than requiring the student to identify the correct table by operational context.
The automated negative_stem flags on true/false and situation_action activities are heuristic misfires — those items test rule recognition in valid formats and do not use exclusion-pattern stems.
Cross-format reinforcement exists for the 8B/RA-event rule (question, flashcard, requirement_check, and situation_action) and for the omission-authority rule (question, flashcard, knowledge_check).
No items cover the strip-waiver conditions (para d.1-d.5) or the FDIO reduced-spacing provision (para e.), leaving those source statements unreinforced in this shard.
Paragraph-location scaffolding persists in one question stem ('under 2-3-5') and in two explanations that reference 'the paragraph' or 'that note' instead of stating the rule directly.

generation_guidance: Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems. The learning objective is operational knowledge, not document structure.

When a source block states a compound rule (e.g., the UAS no-service provision distinguishes model aircraft at any altitude from other UAS at/below 400ft AGL), the explanation should teach the full distinction rather than only the sub-rule matching the correct answer.

Cross-format reinforcement is desirable, but each format instance should access the concept from a distinct retrieval angle — do not restate the same true/false judgment as both a question and an activity.

Thin explanations that merely restate the answer are a systemwide concern. When the source provides additional context (precluding factors, exceptions, operational triggers), use it. When the source states only a bare duty or fact, the explanation should at least locate the concept in its operational context without inventing rationales the source does not support.

When generating multiple-choice distractors for list-based items, ensure all choices are of comparable length and grammatical structure. Avoid formulaic prefixes such as 'Only...' that mark distractors.

For procedural paragraphs with sequential steps, continue the pattern seen here of assigning one focused flashcard per branch of the decision tree rather than one card that dumps the entire procedure.
Cover the uncovered step: para 2-1-10a4 — actions when normal operation is reported after standby equipment activation (continue use, record incident, notify technical operations personnel).

Explanations for procedural items should state the controlling requirement directly from the source. Do not fabricate safety rationales, design justifications, or operational consequences that are absent from the source blocks.
Write explanations that teach the controlling rule using the full source text, not just a restatement of the correct answer choice
Avoid document-structure references such as 'subparagraph,' 'paragraph,' or 'section' in explanations and stems
Continue the cross-format coverage pattern (question + activity + flashcard) for foundational operational rules
Expand the scenario-based activity approach demonstrated by item 00442c5e to other rules
When verifying true/false statements that contain negatives, ensure the explanation teaches the affirmative operational rule

When listing multi-condition requirements as the correct answer, balance distractor length by giving each distractor a parallel set of conditions (even if two rather than three) rather than using short phrases like 'Any two of those three conditions.'

For T/F and binary-check activities where the stem already restates the source rule, write an explanation that identifies the source paragraph and explains the operational distinction rather than labeling it ('This is the rule for X').
Continue the cross-format approach seen in this shard — each core concept (beacon codes, join-up, separation, break-up) should appear in at least two formats.

The standard formation beacon code, nonstandard formation beacon code, and join-up conditions are strong candidates for scenario-based activities that present a formation type and ask the controller to determine the correct beacon-code action.

When the source states only a procedural requirement without elaboration, the explanation should state the requirement fully using the source's own language and connect it to the operational context (e.g., airspace entry, control transfer) — without inventing a safety rationale.
Avoid referencing paragraph designators (subparagraph a, b, c) in stems; rephrase as self-contained operational questions.
Match distractor length to the correct-answer length. When the correct answer must be long because it combines two conditions from the source, either split the item or lengthen the distractors.

Before creating multiple items on the same concept, check whether existing items in other formats already provide retrieval diversity; a knowledge_check activity and a multiple_choice question on the same concept with the same answer set is duplication, not reinforcement.
Flashcard backs must be complete enough to serve as a stand-alone retrieval target. A one-word 'No' or 'Yes' back is insufficient — include the substantive rule.
Remove paragraph-number references from flashcard fronts and activity question_text; use operational descriptions instead
Ensure multiple-choice distractors are similar in length to the correct answer so answer length does not reveal the keyed response
Provide substantive flashcard backs that restate the concept, not just 'Yes' or 'No'
Avoid pairing a true/false item with a multiple-choice item on the same source statement within a single paragraph's item set; choose the stronger format
Frame scope-check activities around whether a rule applies in a described operational situation, not whether a paragraph enumerates a list of topics
Write multiple-choice distractors at roughly the same length and specificity as the correct answer — a correct choice that is 2–3x longer than all distractors is a reliable answer-length cue.

Explanations must teach the controlling operational rule or condition, not merely repeat the answer text or cite a subparagraph identifier. For exact-regulatory-value items (e.g., 5 minutes), the explanation should note where the value comes from and its operational scope.

Avoid placing the same concept in two items of near-identical format (e.g., a true/false question and a conditional_rule_check activity both testing the monitor-on-ground rule). Cross-format reinforcement works when formats differ materially (MCQ vs flashcard vs scenario activity).
The fill-in-the-blank format is appropriate for prescribed phraseology recall but should still carry a teaching explanation beyond restating the answer.

Future content generation should consider adding items for: location name omission rules for same-facility or shared-name transfers; the ERAM voice communication indicator requirement; and the numbers-preceding-decimal omission for ground control frequencies in the 121 MHz band.
Ensure every question explanation is at least 10 words and teaches the specific conditions from the source (e.g., 'time permitting,' 'when necessary') rather than restating the correct answer.
Review stems for ambiguous placement words like 'follow' that can be read as asking about aftermath rather than internal structure.
Maintain the current cross-format balance where all four response options in the paragraph appear as questions, activities, and flashcards.
Continue using scenario-based activities with plausible peer-pressure or procedural-confusion distractors — they test operational judgment effectively.

When the source body text and phraseology differ in level of detail (e.g., body says 'restrictions followed by APPROVED' while phraseology includes the requested operation), explanations can reference the phraseology structure without inventing rationales.
Eliminate paragraph references from all future stems, card fronts, and explanations unless the item is a source-reference retrieval card.
Write explanations that teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception. Avoid explanations that merely restate the correct answer, describe the item's format, or cite a paragraph number.

Limit coverage of any single source statement to at most one item per retrieval format (question, activity, flashcard). Split multi-concept items rather than repeating the same narrow rule across formats.
For T/F items, craft the false condition around a genuine operational misconception rather than a trivial negation of the rule.
Flashcard backs must stand alone as retrieval targets. A back like 'To touchdown' requires the front's context to be meaningful and fails as a reverse cue.
Replace paragraph-number references with operational framing in all flashcard fronts; use the concept itself (e.g., 'the judgment-based caution rule') instead of 'in 2-1-20'.
Ensure all choices in knowledge_check and multiple_choice activities are structurally parallel in length and complexity, especially when the correct answer is compound.

The note block's two key ideas (controller not responsible for anticipating wake; Super/Heavy flight path increases encounter chance) are tested by only one flashcard (bcfb14db). Consider a second retrieval mode for the 'increased chance' element.

The body block's requirement to include 'position, altitude if known, and direction of flight' in the advisory is exercised only implicitly by the phraseology_builder. A dedicated question or flashcard could make this explicit.

The two thin_back Super/Heavy phraseology cards could be combined into a single card asking 'What words must be included in a wake turbulence advisory based on known aircraft class?' with a back listing both rules.
For phraseology_builder activities involving mileage, always include 'ZERO' in the word bank and use group-form numbers (ONE ZERO, not TEN) per FAA standard number usage in traffic advisories.
When the correct answer for a multiple_choice or knowledge_check item is inherently compound, ensure at least one distractor is comparably detailed to avoid answer-length cues.
Do not create fill_blank questions with a single accepted answer that is one word; use multiple_choice with plausible unauthorized-phraseology distractors to test recognition of correct phraseology.
Flashcard backs must provide enough operational detail to serve as standalone retrieval targets; 'Issue the indicated altitude' is too thin for a card whose front asks about altitude wording.
The Mode C not-radar-identified subparagraph (2-1-21c) is covered by only one flashcard; add a second retrieval mode (question or activity) to reinforce the 'altitude indicates' phrasing.
The note block listing relative-movement terms (closing, converging, parallel, opposite, diverging, overtaking, crossing) is untested by any item; consider a terminology-recall card or activity.
Do not generate items that test which specific word, number, or aircraft type appeared in an illustrative phraseology example. Examples are instructional models, not exam material.
Never open a stem with 'Under 2-1-XX' or cite a paragraph number in an explanation. The student needs to learn the rule and its operational application, not its document location.
When the source provides a flat requirement without an explicit reason, state the requirement directly in the explanation. Do not infer a safety rationale, policy purpose, or operational consequence.

For two-paragraph shards with similar but distinct rules (e.g., UAS vs bird advisories), generate items that test the distinctions between them — different detail sets, different continuation rules (the 'unless' clause in bird advisories), and different relay/coordination requirements — rather than generating duplicate items from each paragraph in isolation.

Cross-format coverage should test different facets of the same rule, not the same facet in a different wrapper. A knowledge_check activity that asks the same question as a multiple_choice question adds no new retrieval path.

When generating distractors for detail-set questions, use the other detail set from the same or adjacent paragraph as a plausible incorrect answer (e.g., omitting 'distance' for known-UAS, or including 'distance' for reported-UAS). This tests whether the student genuinely distinguishes the two.

Phraseology-builder activities were well-executed in this shard. Continue providing accurate word banks that include plausible distractors drawn from standard ATC vocabulary, with correct sequences verified against the source examples.

For the bird-activity relay requirement in 2-1-23b, consider generating an activity that asks WHEN to relay rather than just whether relaying is required — this tests the conditional judgment ('whenever it appears it will become a factor') rather than mere recall of the relay duty.

Never open a stem with 'Under 2-1-XX,' 'Per 2-1-XX,' or 'in 2-1-XX.' Never cite a paragraph number in an explanation. The student is learning ATC rules and procedures, not document organization. State the rule directly without locating it.

When writing explanations for fill-in-the-blank phraseology questions, teach the full prescribed phraseology and its operational context, not just which word fills the blank. For example, instead of 'Per 2-1-27, the approved instruction uses CONTACT,' explain the complete Brasher notification format and when it is issued.

When the correct answer for a knowledge-check activity is substantially longer than distractors because it lists multiple categories or conditions, either shorten the correct answer to the governing principle (e.g., 'Any condition that impacts sector or position operations' instead of the full enumerated list) or lengthen distractors to comparable word counts. The correct answer must not be identifiable by visual scan alone.

For phraseology-builder activities, the explanation should connect the built transmission to the operational rule — when it is issued, what it means, and any conditions or limitations. Do not merely state 'The correct transmission is [target phrase].'

For paragraphs with single-source-block content (2-1-24, 2-1-26), generate a mix of formats: a rule-trigger question (when does it apply), a governance question (what controls it), and a T/F item testing a common operational misconception. The flashcard should target the core retrieval task — trigger condition or governing authority.

For paragraphs with phraseology content (2-1-25, 2-1-27), prioritize the operational conditions and distinctions: when tower issues the check vs. when approach/GCA issues it, what triggers a deviation notification, the Brasher terminology prohibition. Do not generate items that test which word completes an isolated phraseology fragment without teaching the surrounding rule.

Cross-format reinforcement works well when the question, activity, and flashcard each test a different facet of the same rule, not the same facet in different wrappers. Avoid generating a knowledge_check activity that asks the same question as an existing multiple_choice question.
The flashcards in this shard demonstrate the right pattern: front poses a specific operational question, back gives a concise rule-based answer without paragraph citations. Continue this pattern.

Spot-the-error activities should use authentic phraseology errors drawn from the source's prescribed wording — the POSSIBLE/PROBABLE substitution in this shard is a good example of a realistic, operationally meaningful error.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems; begin with the operational situation or concept (e.g., 'After an aircraft reports it is responding to a TCAS RA...' not 'Under 2-1-28...').

Never cite paragraph numbers in explanations. Teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception directly from the source text. If the source states only a duty or requirement, explain that requirement — do not add unsupported rationale.

When a correct answer must list multiple conditions (e.g., three TCAS RA separation-resumption triggers), expand distractors to comparable length to reduce answer-length cuing. Do not shorten the correct answer at the expense of accuracy.
Verify sub-paragraph letter references against the specific source edition used. The packet source blocks in this shard label the separation-responsibility content as paragraph 'c,' not 'd.'

For table_lookup activities, ensure the task requires consulting the referenced table to reach an operational decision. Do not reduce the activity to identifying which table a source paragraph references.
When constructing fill_blank items from published phraseology examples, preserve the exact punctuation of the source, including commas.

Cross-reference source blocks for exact capitalization: the source uses 'negative RVSM' in body text but the fill_blank item tests the uppercase 'NEGATIVE' token; both conventions are consistent with their respective contexts (prose vs. all-caps transmission display).

Never embed paragraph numbers in stems or explanations. Teach the controlling rule, condition, or exception directly from source text. For example, write 'Controllers must not issue control instructions contrary to the TAWS procedure the crew is executing' not 'Per 2-1-30a, controllers must not...'

When a correct answer must list multiple enumerated conditions (e.g., three TAWS separation-resumption triggers), expand distractors to comparable length and specificity. Do not pair a three-condition correct answer with single-clause distractors.

Check for cross-format duplication before generating items. The same operational fact tested via the same retrieval mode (e.g., two multiple-choice items with identical stems) should not appear in both the question and activity pools.

Flashcard fronts must identify the concept being recalled without referencing paragraph numbers. Ask 'What operational principle determines which procedure to prefer?' not 'What principle drives the choices in 2-1-3?'

When summarizing multiple conditions on a flashcard back, ensure the grouping is unambiguous. Use punctuation or explicit scoping (e.g., a parenthetical or list numbering) so the learner knows which conditions carry additional requirements like observation of reestablished separation.

For TAWS content: the source is operationally rich with specific do/don't duties, condition lists for resumption, and explicit suspension of separation responsibility. Generate items that test application of these boundary conditions, not just recall of the phrase 'do not issue contrary instructions.'

Spread coverage across the full priority catalog instead of concentrating on MEDEVAC and AIR EVAC/HOSP triggers. At minimum, test the unique activation conditions for SCOOT (landline or air/ground comms, not in flight ID), Garden Plot (CARF authorization required), and NRP (not subject to route-limiting restrictions).

When multiple items cover the same fact, vary the retrieval mode meaningfully. A question that asks 'when does X receive priority' and an activity that asks the same question with the same distractors is not cross-format reinforcement — it is duplication.

Avoid referencing paragraph numbers in stems. The baseline service-order question is valid content but should stand alone as 'What is the baseline service order before the listed priority exceptions are applied?'

Explanations should connect the specific rule to the operational framework. For simple listed exceptions like IFR-over-SVFR, explain that it is one of the explicit exceptions to the first-come, first-served baseline, not merely that it is a listed exception.

The situation_action format works well for 2-1-4 because priority decisions are inherently situational. More scenario-based activities that force the controller to choose between competing priority claims would strengthen the chapter.
True/false items work for binary operational triggers (verbal request required, remarks alone not sufficient) but are overused when multiple T/F items test the same trigger from different angles.

Explanations for this paragraph should state both the immediately and expedite thresholds explicitly rather than identifying only which one applies to a scenario. The student needs to learn the rule, not just the answer.

Flashcard backs should include at least a brief definition even when the front provides scenario context. A back such as 'Expedite — prompt compliance to avoid the development of an imminent situation' supports both retrieval directions.

The expedite/immediately distinction is the core learning objective. Generate application scenarios that require choosing between the two words based on whether the situation is already imminent or still developing.

The expedite cancellation rule (changed/restated altitude without renewed expedite cancels the instruction) is a narrow but important exception that merits retention across question and flashcard formats.
Avoid automated negative_stem flags on 'which word fits' stems — these are positive discrimination questions, not negative stems.
Balance correct-answer and distractor lengths within 2–3 words of each other; runtime shuffling does not remedy length-based cues.
For phraseology fill-in-the-blank items, include operational context in the explanation — connect the prescribed word to the alert type and trigger condition from the source paragraph.
Prefer multiple-choice over true/false when the tested rule is a prohibition ('do not assume'); ask what the controller must do rather than whether a prohibited action is permitted.

Avoid testing the same concept in the same retrieval mode across questions and activities within one shard; use activities to test supplementary dimensions (factors, conditions, procedural steps) not already covered by a question.
Deduplicate word-bank entries in phraseology_builder activities; a word that appears N times in the target phrase needs only one entry in the bank, and the correct_sequence may reuse the same index.
Never reference paragraph numbers in question stems, flashcard fronts, or explanations. Test whether the student knows the rule or example, not where it lives in the document.

Do not create reasoning-type flashcards unless the source blocks explicitly state a reason. Para 2-1-7 states controller duties but does not explain why they exist; prefer procedure, definition, example, and requirement card types for this material.

The note block in 2-1-7 contains the definition and examples of inflight equipment malfunctions. Ensure students learn the scope of what counts as a malfunction before testing application of the assistance and relay duties in paras b and c.

When a source block states only a duty, threshold, or coordination requirement, explain the requirement directly. Do not add claims about why the duty exists or what operational problem it averts unless the source makes that claim explicitly.

Do not generate a knowledge_check activity whose stem and correct answer duplicate an existing multiple-choice question in the same paragraph. Cross-format reinforcement requires a different retrieval mode, not the same stem in a different activity wrapper.
Flashcard backs answering yes/no questions should include the source statement as contextual reinforcement, not a bare yes/no answer alone.
Flashcard fronts should self-identify the operational scenario. A card about handoff procedures should mention minimum fuel so the student does not need to infer the context from the deck grouping.
Continue the strong pattern of plausible operational distractors (omitting required notification, converting advisory to emergency prematurely, conditioning notification on pilot request).
Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems or flashcard fronts; describe the operational situation directly.
Do not generate reasoning cards that infer rationale, sequential dependencies, or actor motivations not explicitly stated in the source blocks.
Flashcard backs should be concise but self-contained — bare acronyms stripped of all context defeat the retrieval purpose.
Activity explanations must teach the controlling operational principle, not merely restate that the answer matches the paragraph.
For short paragraphs like 2-1-9 the concept pool is small; limit items to one per concept per format and use cross-format reinforcement deliberately rather than generating filler items.

The BASOPS military overseas flight note (source block 1, first two sentences) is entirely untested. Consider items testing when a pilot would file directly with an FAA facility versus through BASOPS, and that BASOPS forwards IFR flight notification messages to the appropriate center.
Avoid triple-covering the same verbatim directive in the same retrieval mode; limit the authorized abbreviations concept to one question and one activity per shard.
When the correct answer is a multi-part list (strip, data entry, voice recorder), ensure all distractors have comparable word count to avoid answer-length cues.

For MC questions testing what should be used during a procedure, use distractors that represent plausible incorrect operational practices a new controller might choose (e.g., writing full text for clarity, using facility-level codes instead of FAA-authorized abbreviations).
Continue the pattern of including one applied-scenario activity and one reasoning flashcard per paragraph group; this shard's situation_action and reasoning card are effective models.

Paragraph 2-2-10 contains two distinct operational modes — ensure questions about the PBCT / Computer B rule always include the inter-center computer operation condition to prevent conflation with nonautomated NADIN procedures.
When explaining numeric thresholds, state the complete operational consequence (e.g., 'NADIN to all affected centers') rather than only repeating the threshold value.
Flashcard fronts should identify concepts by their operational function (e.g., 'In flight plan routing, what is an element?') rather than by paragraph number.
The three nonautomated NADIN trigger conditions are distinct and each deserves its own retrieval item; do not collapse them into a single question that tests only which-number-is-bigger trivia.

Explanations must teach the underlying prescribed phraseology pattern or procedural requirement from the source blocks, not merely repeat the correct answer text. For fill_blank items testing phraseology, include the full phraseology template so the learner understands the pattern.

Balance distractor and correct-answer lengths so the correct choice is not identifiable by word count or specificity alone. When the correct answer necessarily contains multiple operational elements, expand distractors to comparable detail.

When the same concept appears in both question and activity formats, ensure the activity uses a genuinely different retrieval mode — construction, judgment, phraseology building — rather than duplicating multiple-choice selection under a different type label.

For this paragraph, use phraseology builder activities more broadly to reinforce the prescribed transmission templates shown in the source examples, and consider adding flashcards for the 3-minute ETA threshold and the 30-minute coordination exception.
Never embed paragraph numbers in stems, fronts, or question_text. Use operational who/what/where phrasing instead.
Match distractor length and specificity to the correct answer so the item cannot be solved by pattern recognition alone.
Construct distractors from genuine ATC concepts that a controller might reasonably confuse — other items in the same paragraph, adjacent rules, or common operational misconceptions.
Flashcard backs should be complete retrieval targets, not single words or bare facility names. Combine closely related facts into one card only when the combined fact is the retrieval target.
Avoid generating multiple flashcards that test the same source fact from only a slightly different angle. Each flashcard should target a distinct concept.
Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems. Replace 'in 2-2-13' with self-contained operational framing such as 'under the international forwarding rules' or equivalent context.

Balance choice lengths when the correct answer is a compound condition. Inherent complexity of a two-part rule does not excuse a correct answer that is twice as long as the longest distractor. Shorten the correct answer to its essential components and lengthen distractors to similar character counts.

Explanations must teach the controlling rule rather than restate the answer. For exclusion questions (e.g. 'which are excluded'), state the full forwarding obligation and then identify the exclusion category — do not merely paraphrase the answer choice.
Continue the cross-format reinforcement pattern seen here. Domestic, international, and return-to-U.S. forwarding rules each benefit from paired question + activity coverage.
The situation_action format is well-suited to paragraphs with multiple coordinated obligations. The item fc5563be demonstrates effective multi-rule scenario construction.

Remove all paragraph cross-references from stems, backs, explanations, and activity content. State the operational procedure directly (e.g., 'fields 1 through 8 of IFR Flight Progress Data' instead of 'fields 1 through 8 from 2-2-6a').
Explanations must teach the controlling rule or procedure, not merely restate the answer. For format rules, explain which format applies under which condition and what procedure follows.
Do not use fill_blank for numeric paragraph-trivia answers. Use multiple_choice with plausible operational distractors when testing procedural knowledge.
Avoid negative stems unless exclusion recognition is the actual tested skill. A positive stem ('what message type do Canadian ACCs transmit in addition to...') tests the same knowledge more directly.
Cross-format reinforcement exists for the strip-form overflow concept (Q2, Q5, A1, A5, F5, F6) but the Canadian correction-messages concept needs a matching activity for full three-format coverage.
Do not reference section titles (e.g., 'TELETYPE FLIGHT DATA FORMAT') in question stems or activity content. Test the operational scope directly.

For paragraph 2-2-15, the four NRP rules are short and well-suited to rote recall. Future generation should test application by having the learner distinguish whether a given route change is tactical (retain NRP) or pilot-requested (remove NRP).

Explanations should contrast the triggering condition against the opposite rule rather than restating the answer — for example, emphasize that the reason for the route change (tactical vs. pilot-requested) controls whether NRP is retained or removed.
Avoid manufacturing distractors from unrelated acronyms or concepts absent from the source paragraph.
Do not generate scope/paragraph-location items for this paragraph; the substance is the NRP remark handling rules.
Do not ask 'why' questions about distinctions the source text states without explanation — rephrase as operational recall instead.

For procedural paragraphs like 2-2-2 that lack stated rationale, structure explanations to contrast the rule with its exception or to connect related source blocks rather than repeating the answer alone.

When writing scope_check activities, use unambiguous directional language ('forwarded to', 'are sent to') rather than agency-ambiguous phrasing ('responsibility belongs to') to prevent confusion about who performs the action versus where it is directed.

Avoid producing two activities that test the same source statement in closely related formats; prefer one activity per concept and reinforce across formats (question, activity, flashcard) rather than within the same format.
The conditional_rule_check activity type is well-suited to this paragraph's EAS FDP exception logic; future generation should use it deliberately for other paragraphs with similar conditional rules.

Do not use paragraph-heading or paragraph-structure language in explanations; convey operational responsibility instead. 'Terminal controllers are responsible for...' not 'The paragraph is a terminal-only rule.'
Ensure stems are self-contained without pronouns that refer to absent context ('this rule,' 'this forwarding rule').
Prefer positive conditional constructions for rule-check items; reserve negative stems for exclusion-recognition skills that genuinely require identifying what is excluded.
Explanations must teach the controlling rule, not merely restate the answer or identify a paragraph label.

When source material is very limited (two sentences), conserve item count and maximize retrieval diversity across formats rather than generating multiple items that test the same fact in the same mode.

Explanations must teach the controlling rule, not merely restate the answer or quote the source phrase. For 2-2-4, explain that terminal controllers must forward departure times to military operations for all DVFR joint-use departures, and that civil pilots separately must include 'DVFR to (destination)' on initial call-up.

Avoid paragraph-number references in flashcard fronts, question stems, and activity prompts. Reference the operational concept directly (e.g., 'DVFR departure-time forwarding requirement and civil pilot call-up phrase') instead of 'the main rule and the note in 2-2-4.'

For situation-action activities that address two independent rules, balance choice lengths so the correct answer does not stand out as the longest option. Either compress the correct answer or ensure distractors carry comparable explanatory weight.

When source material is very limited (one body rule plus two notes), conserve item count and prioritize retrieval diversity across formats rather than generating thin items that add no instructional value beyond what the stem already communicates.
Do not flag thin_back on phraseology or definition flashcards whose back is a single prescribed term or phrase — concise backs are appropriate for exact-recall targets.
For single-sentence source paragraphs, limit total item generation to 4-6 items across all formats to prevent redundancy.
Never embed paragraph numbers in flashcard fronts; use operational context to identify the concept being recalled.
For directive_check activities, present an incorrect operational directive — not the source text verbatim — so the student must evaluate correctness rather than recognize a quote.
Do not generate scope_check or true/false items that merely restate a rule's trigger condition and ask whether it applies.
Fill_blank items for single-word completions from very short paragraphs are acceptable only when the blank tests a defined term essential to the operational procedure.
Explanations for one-sentence rules should state the complete operational requirement including the trigger condition, not merely rephrase the answer choice.

When a paragraph contains a dense enumerated list (13 items in 2-2-6), prioritize testing the operational conditions and exceptions that affect controller decision-making rather than attempting to quiz every list element.

Do not generate a knowledge_check activity that duplicates the same retrieval mode as an existing multiple-choice question for the same concept; use different activity types (directive_check, situation_action, conditional_rule_check) to achieve genuine cross-format reinforcement.

Match distractor length and specificity to the correct answer. When the correct answer is a multi-word phrase like 'the remarks section of flight progress strips,' construct distractors of comparable detail.

For cards flagged with paragraph-location scaffolding, remove the paragraph reference entirely rather than replacing it with a generic alternative. The front must identify the concept through its operational trigger alone.
Explanations should teach the operational requirement — when it applies, what triggers it, and how it relates to other procedures in the paragraph — not merely restate the correct answer choice.

The situation_action activity (c1c8854e) in this shard is a strong example: it combines two procedural requirements in a realistic operational scenario requiring the student to recognize both violations and select the correct multi-part response.
For single-sentence paragraphs, cap item count proportionally to source depth. This shard has 15 items for one sentence; the last few items inevitably recycle the same concept.
Never embed paragraph numbers in question stems or flashcard fronts. The learning target is the operational rule, not its organizational location.
Explanations must state the complete rule (condition + action), not merely echo the question's subject. A thin explanation adding no information beyond the stem is a defect.

When multiple activity types are generated for the same thin source, ensure each type demands a meaningfully distinct cognitive response, not just a different label and instruction wrapper around the same true/false statement.

In situation_action activities, never include a para_context or hint field that recites the source rule. The situation should present the operational scenario; the student must derive the action from their knowledge.
For single-sentence source paragraphs, prefer two or three tightly focused formats over five each of questions, cards, and activities. Fewer items with higher discrimination avoid forced duplication.
Ensure multiple-choice distractors match the correct answer in specificity and length. When the correct answer enumerates three purposes, make each distractor comparably detailed.
Flashcard fronts must name the operational concept, never the paragraph number. A learner in the field recalls the rule, not the reference.
Avoid restating a rule in both positive and negative form as separate activities. Choose the form that matches the source's own phrasing.

True/false explanations must state the controlling rule from the source, not merely confirm the answer. A direct restatement of the source block is acceptable when rationale is absent from the source.

Do not embed paragraph numbers in stems, flashcard fronts, or activity prompts. Describe the operational condition instead (e.g., 'when transferring control information by computer message without automatic acknowledgment' rather than 'under 2-2-9').
Balance distractor lengths against the correct answer. When the correct answer must enumerate multiple alternatives, write distractors of comparable length and complexity to avoid answer-length cues.
Provide explanations of at least ten words that state the controlling rule, condition, or exception directly. Avoid restating the answer or merely naming the source paragraph.

Eliminate fill_blank items that duplicate concepts already covered by a stronger multiple-choice or activity item in the same paragraph set. A single well-constructed multiple-choice item with plausible distractors is more educationally valuable than a fill_blank recall item.
Preserve the situation_action format for operational scenarios; it is the most educationally valuable activity type for testing decision-making under realistic conditions.

Create items covering uncovered source statements: manual strip conformance to machine format, slant-line zero and underline-S conditions, and slant-line zero requirement on weather data. These are substantive operational requirements not yet represented in any format.

When generating explanations for procedural items, state the required action directly from the source rather than reaching for inferred rationales. If the source provides no stated purpose, do not supply one.

Prefer specific markings and method names over generic phrases like 'approved method' in procedure flashcards and questions, unless the tested skill is the overall process rather than the exact marking.

Cross-reference between the two paragraph objects sharing para_id 2-3-1 to avoid further duplication; the second object's flashcard set should be treated as separate-target coverage rather than re-covering the same ground.

When the source paragraph is a reference/authorization hub (lists of permitted tools, tables, and figures), do not convert every abbreviation into a separate drill item — cluster related symbols and ask the learner to apply them in an operational context.
Explanations for 2-3-10 items should state which of the 'you may use' alternatives applies, what condition triggers it, and what restriction governs it — not merely repeat the answer.
Distractors drawn from the same source table should match the correct answer in length and specificity; short buzzword distractors create answer-length cues.
Replace all paragraph-number references with descriptive labels (e.g., 'the Clearance Abbreviations table' not 'the 2-3-10 clearance abbreviation table').
Give balanced coverage across all four 'you may use' alternatives, not just locally approved identifiers and the strip contingency.

Cross-format reinforcement is healthy, but five items on one restriction (locally approved identifiers) and zero items on control information symbols is imbalanced coverage, not intentional reinforcement.
Explanations for block-assignment questions should reference TBL 2-3-1 and state what the block records, not merely say 'that entry belongs in block N.'
Balance distractor length against the correct answer; avoid 'Only...' prefixes, absolute language, or patterns that reveal non-answers.

Limit coverage to two formats per distinct table fact: one forward (block → content) and one reverse (content → block) across question and flashcard types. A third format (activity) on the same fact is overrepresentation.
Do not place the same multiple-choice item in both question and knowledge_check activity formats; pick one entity type for each retrieval target.
Label tables as 'the en route flight progress strip block table' or 'TBL 2-3-1,' never 'the 2-3-2 table.' Paragraph numbers belong in source metadata, not in item text.

Design table_lookup activities around conditional or scenario-driven lookup decisions (e.g., which block applies to an arrival at a nonapproach control airport) rather than simple one-to-one block-number recall.

When the source provides an operational condition or prerequisite alongside a definition (e.g., 'selectable for aircraft whose flight plans contain the required equipment qualifiers'), include that condition in the explanation so the learner understands when the flag applies, not just what it stands for.
Never embed paragraph numbers (e.g., '2-3-3') in question_text, source_label, task, or instruction fields. Reference sources by descriptive name (e.g., 'oceanic strip data entry table').
For table_lookup activities, verify that the explanation teaches why the table entry matters operationally, not just which value to select.

For this chapter section, flag-definition items should pair the flag letter with both the full name and the acronym (e.g., 'Mach Number Technique (MNT)') to build recognition of the acronyms used elsewhere in the order.
