operations.problem: The explanation is vague and uninformative: 'That special reporting rule applies to those towers.' It neither states what the rule is nor identifies the operational condition that triggers it, leaving the learner with no substantive teaching from the explanation.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 2-6-3d states: 'Towers at airports where military turbo-jet en route descents are routinely conducted must also report the conditions to the ARTCC even if it is not the controlling facility.'

match.question_text: Towers at airports where military turbo-jet en route descents are routinely conducted must report the conditions to the ARTCC even if it is not the controlling facility.

replacements.question_text: Towers at airports where military turbo-jet en route descents are routinely conducted must report the conditions to the ARTCC even if it is not the controlling facility.
replacements.explanation: Towers at airports with routine military turbo-jet en route descents carry an additional reporting obligation: they must forward weather conditions to the ARTCC even when the ARTCC is not the controlling facility for that airspace.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The explanation references the paragraph number ('the calm-wind rule in 2-6-3') instead of teaching the operational rule. The learner gains no substantive understanding from a paragraph-location reference.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 2-6-3b states: 'Describe the wind as calm when the wind velocity is less than three knots.'

match.question_text: Wind velocity less than three knots is described as calm.

replacements.question_text: Wind velocity less than three knots is described as calm.
replacements.explanation: Wind is described as calm when the velocity is less than three knots. This is the threshold used for reporting and describing wind conditions.

choices.text: True
False

operations.problem: The explanation references the paragraph number ('the calm-wind threshold in 2-6-3') instead of restating the operational rule. This teaches the learner where to find the rule rather than what the rule is.
operations.source_basis: Paragraph 2-6-3b states: 'Describe the wind as calm when the wind velocity is less than three knots.'

content.question_text: Controllers must describe the wind as calm when the wind velocity is less than three knots.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: That is the calm-wind threshold in 2-6-3.

content.question_text: Controllers must describe the wind as calm when the wind velocity is less than three knots.

choices.text: True
False

operations.source_basis: Paragraph 2-6-3c states: 'Forward current weather changes to the appropriate control facility as follows: 1. When the official weather changes to a condition: (a) Less than a 1,000-foot ceiling or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is greater. (b) Where the visibility is less than 3 miles. (c) Where conditions improve to values greater than those listed in (a) and (b).'

content.question_text: Which official weather change must be forwarded to the appropriate control facility?

choices.text: A drop to less than a 1,000-foot ceiling or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is greater.
Any cloud cover change, even when visibility and ceiling remain well above thresholds.
A change in dew point alone.
A temperature change of one degree or more.

content.explanation: The reporting triggers include the listed ceiling and visibility thresholds and improvements back above them.

content.question_text: Which official weather change must be forwarded to the appropriate control facility?

choices.text: A drop to less than a 1,000-foot ceiling or below the highest circling minimum, whichever is greater.
Any cloud cover change, even when visibility and ceiling remain well above thresholds.
A change in dew point alone, regardless of current ceiling or visibility conditions.
A temperature change of one degree or more, regardless of ceiling or visibility conditions.

content.explanation: Weather changes that must be forwarded to the appropriate control facility include a drop below a 1,000-foot ceiling or below the highest circling minimum (whichever is greater), visibility dropping below 3 miles, and improvements back above those thresholds. Changes in temperature or dew point alone do not trigger the forwarding requirement.

operations.problem: The stem references 'the note' as a document-location scaffold instead of asking about the operational principle directly. The question tests understanding of transponder operation under high barometric pressure, which should be presented as an operational concept rather than a document annotation.
operations.source_basis: Note 1: 'Altitude reporting transponders transmit the pressure altitude (Flight Level) of the aircraft. The altimeter setting does not directly affect the transponder reported altitude. ATC automation applies the current altimeter setting to the pressure altitude received and displays the altitude of the aircraft above mean sea level (MSL).' Note 2: 'With a barometric pressure of 31.30" Hg and the aircraft's altimeter set at 31.00" Hg, the Mode C transponder will transmit the actual altitude of 3,300, feet while the altimeter only shows 3,000 feet.'

match.question_text: Why does the note explain that high barometric pressure can create a difference between transponder altitude and the cockpit altimeter?

replacements.question_text: Why can high barometric pressure create a difference between the transponder-reported altitude and the cockpit altimeter indication?
replacements.explanation: Altitude reporting transponders transmit pressure altitude. ATC automation applies the current altimeter setting to the pressure altitude received and displays altitude above MSL. The altimeter setting does not directly affect the transponder-reported altitude. When the actual barometric pressure differs from the altimeter setting, the cockpit altimeter and the ATC display can diverge.

choices.text: Because the transponder reports pressure altitude while ATC automation applies the altimeter setting to display altitude above MSL.
Because Mode C directly transmits the pilot's selected local altimeter setting.
Because the final approach fix automatically resets the transponder to local pressure.
Because airports above 31.00 inches mercury are required to shut down altitude-reporting transponders.

False

content.explanation: That is the departure rule when the barometric pressure exceeds 31.00 inches mercury.

content.question_text: During high barometric pressure procedures, departures must set 31.00 prior to reaching any mandatory or crossing altitude or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.

choices.text: True
False

content.explanation: When barometric pressure exceeds 31.00 inches mercury, departing aircraft must set the altimeter to 31.00 before reaching the first mandatory or crossing altitude, or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever occurs at a lower altitude.

By omitting the runway number and stating only the limiting value.

operations.problem: This scope_check activity tests the same concept as question f90ddc29 (RVR placement within the weather reporting sequence) using the same multiple-choice retrieval mode. operations.source_basis: When issued along with other weather elements, transmit these values in the normal sequence used for weather reporting.

It is transmitted only after the altimeter setting regardless of the rest of the sequence.

Issue only the current value and omit the variability range.
State the average of the variable values instead of the current reading.
Stop issuing RVR because variable values are not reportable.

content.situation: A runway RVR readout is rapidly varying by 1,000 feet or more. Another controller wants to issue only the current value because adding the range sounds too wordy.

choices.text: Report the current value followed by the range of visibility variance.
Issue only the current value and omit the variability range.
State the average of the variable values instead of the current reading.

operations.problem: The stem contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('from 2-9-2') and the fill-in-the-blank format tests recall of a single code letter ('CHARLIE') from an example rather than the underlying operational procedure. The explanation is extremely thin and does not teach the controlling rule. The item reduces to trivia — which letter an example happened to use — instead of testing what a controller must do when a pilot has not stated the ATIS code on initial contact.
operations.source_basis: Source blocks sequence 1 and 2: Controllers must ensure that pilots receive the most current pertinent information. When a pilot does not state the appropriate ATIS code on initial contact, the controller asks the pilot to confirm receipt of the current ATIS information. Example phraseology includes 'Verify you have information CHARLIE' and 'Information CHARLIE current. Advise when you have CHARLIE.'

replacements.question_text: When a pilot does not state the ATIS code on initial contact, what must the controller do?

choices.text: Ask the pilot to confirm receipt of the current ATIS information.
Issue a new ATIS recording immediately.
Read the full ATIS broadcast to the pilot verbatim.
Assume the pilot has the current ATIS and proceed normally.

operations.problem: The front text contains paragraph-location scaffolding ('under 2-9-2'). The reference to the paragraph number is unnecessary — the front should be self-contained and frame the question in operational terms. The back content is accurate and the retrieval target is appropriate.

replacements.back: Any pertinent change such as runway change, instrument approach in use, or new or canceled NOTAM or PIREP information.

operations.source_basis: Paragraph 2-10-2 a states 'There are no absolute divisions of responsibilities regarding position operations. The tasks to be completed remain the same whether one, two, or three people are working positions within a facility/sector. The team, as a whole, has responsibility for the safe and efficient operation of that facility/sector.'

content.question_text: What is the basic operating concept for a terminal radar or nonradar team?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility, and the team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the facility or sector.
The radar position alone owns facility operation once aircraft are on frequency.
The radar associate owns coordination and separation whenever more than one controller is working.
Responsibilities are reduced when one person works the position because some tasks no longer apply.

content.explanation: The tasks remain the same regardless of staffing, and the team as a whole is responsible for safe and efficient operation.

operations.source_basis: Paragraph 2-10-3 a: 'There are no absolute divisions of responsibilities regarding position operations. The tasks to be completed remain the same whether one, two, or three people are working positions within a facility/sector. The team, as a whole, has responsibility for the safe and efficient operation of that facility/sector.'

content.question_text: What is the basic operating concept for a tower team?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility, and the tower team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the facility or sector.

content.question_text: What is the basic operating concept for a tower team?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility; the team owns safe and efficient operation.
The local controller alone owns all tower tasks whenever traffic is moving.
When staffed, the coordinator takes over all duties from the other positions.
Only flight data or clearance delivery handles cab objectives in light traffic.

content.explanation: The tasks to be completed remain the same regardless of staffing, and the team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the facility.
